problem_summary: 2019 MCM/ICM Problem D: design an emergency evacuation model for the Louvre that (1) gives staff a range of options to evacuate visitors, (2) identifies bottlenecks limiting exit movement, (3) lets emergency responders enter quickly, (4) adapts to a broad range of threat types, and (5) generalizes to other large crowded structures. The team frames it as a time-stepped maximum-flow routing problem on a node graph of the building, where edge capacities are dynamically recomputed from crowd-flow speed physics. Reported results: 6,700 people fully evacuated in 288.7s of simulated time, computed in 36.15s at 0.1s resolution; the toy validation case (770 people) solved in 0.457s.
models_used: N&M (Nelson & Mowrer) linear speed-density model: v = k - 0.266kD, with channel-type-specific coefficient k (k=1.0-1.23 for stairs by riser/tread geometry, k=1.4 for corridors/aisles/ramps/doorways) — used for corridor and staircase capacity

Stable Evacuation Speed (SES) model: a Newton's-second-law dynamic equation for escape velocity integrated to v(rho1,rho2) = vm(alpha*A + beta*B + gamma), accounting for Front-Crowding (logarithmic spacing term) and Side-Crowding (frictional damping), used for high-density room exits where the linear N&M model breaks down

Graph-theoretic building abstraction: rooms/halls as nodes, corridors/stairs as edges, with two virtual nodes added — a super-source (node 1) carrying remaining-occupancy on edges to each room and a super-sink (node 9) representing the outside, collapsing a multi-source-multi-sink problem into single-source-single-sink
(n+2)x(n+2) overall capacity matrix G = Cq + Sq + En + Ex (corridor-flow, staircase-flow, initial-occupancy, exit-flow submatrices) encoding the whole state
Ford-Fulkerson maximum-flow algorithm (BFS augmenting-path / Edmonds-Karp-style) as the per-timestep routing core, justified by max-flow-min-cut

A custom 'dynamic system planning algorithm' wrapping Ford-Fulkerson in an outer time-step iteration: each step runs max-flow, derives flow & residual (margin) matrices, then recomputes channel/exit capacities from current density via the N&M/SES speed models and updates source occupancy — repeated until remaining people < 0.5

Emergency sub-routine: zero the in-degree of a hazard room (e.g. room on fire), force-transfer its occupants to adjacent safe rooms, compute transfer time T_T, and splice that into the general iteration
key_techniques: Two-virtual-node trick to reduce multi-source/multi-sink evacuation to a single max-flow instance — the central modeling move that makes Ford-Fulkerson directly applicable

Coupling microscopic crowd physics to macroscopic flow: edge capacities are not static but recomputed every timestep from local density using N&M (low/medium density) and SES (high density at exits), giving the static max-flow algorithm time-varying, density-dependent capacities
Time-stepped / iterative re-solve (5s steps in validation, 0.1s for Louvre) turning a static optimization into a dynamic evacuation trajectory

Residual ('Flow Margin') matrix reuse: the leftover capacity from Ford-Fulkerson is repurposed as a bottleneck detector — fully-loaded edges (margin=0) are flagged red and visualized to locate and re-route around bottlenecks

Taxonomy of three distinct bottleneck mechanisms (channel supply-demand imbalance, uneven exit distribution, single-room arch-blocking at doorways) each with a concrete remedy (widen channel, redistribute exits, place a cylindrical obstacle before the exit)
Hierarchical multi-floor assembly: each Louvre floor (2F,1F,0F,-1F,-2F) modeled as its own node graph, then stacked and linked by inter-floor 'stair channels' into one 3D spatial node graph

Speed-of-computation as a feature argument: solving far faster than real evacuation lets staff pre-plan and try many scenarios — explicitly contrasted against slower cellular-automaton/social-force simulations
why_it_won: "It chose the right altitude for the question. Problem D rewards a whole-building, decision-support view, and this team delivered exactly that with a max-flow network model rather than getting lost in an agent-level simulation. The standout move is the principled bridge between scales: instead of treating edge capacities as constants (the typical undergraduate max-flow paper), they feed real crowd-dynamics physics (N&M and SES) into the capacities and re-solve each timestep — so a classic, defensible algorithm (Ford-Fulkerson, with a correctness argument via max-flow-min-cut) acquires genuine dynamics. Every one of the five required deliverables is answered concretely and traceably to a model artifact: bottlenecks fall out of the residual matrix for free; emergency-responder entry comes from monitoring channel utilization dropping to 60-70%; adaptability is demonstrated with a worked room-fire case; generalization is shown by scaling the toy 8-node graph up to the real 5-floor Louvre. It also closes the loop a judge looks for: a toy validation case first, then the real application, runtimes, a sensitivity analysis (±5% density error), honest strengths/weaknesses, and even working MATLAB source in the appendix. The narrative is engineered top-down with two early roadmap figures (problem mind-map + method pipeline) so a judge grasps the whole approach in 30 seconds."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet states concrete results up front (770-person toy case in 0.457s; 6,700 people / 288.7s evacuation computed in 36.15s) — quantified, not vague. Section 1.3 'Overview of our work' plus two roadmap figures (Fig 1 problem mind-map, Fig 2 method pipeline) give the reader the full arc before any math. Microscopic section (4) cleanly separates N&M (general channels) from SES (congested exits) and is explicit about WHY N&M is swapped out at high density — showing model selection judgment. The graph-construction section (5) walks the virtual-node simplification visually (Fig 3) and builds the block matrix G = Cq+Sq+En+Ex (Fig 4) so the data structure is legible. Validation section (6) is the strongest: it reproduces realistic evacuation phases, then dissects three named bottleneck types each with a labeled flow/margin chart and a fix, plus an adaptive fire scenario and an emergency-personnel-entry policy. Louvre application (7) layers per-floor node graphs into a 3D stacked graph. Conclusion (9) splits strengths/weaknesses cleanly for both the model and the algorithm, and names the real Ford-Fulkerson non-termination caveat. Appendix ships the actual BFS/Ford-Fulkerson/Channel/Stairs/Main MATLAB code."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Heavy dependence on real-time density sensing: the dynamic capacity update only works if channel/exit densities are measured live (the paper admits this as a weakness and assumes density detectors everywhere) — limits real-world deployability

The Louvre 'data' is essentially fabricated/assumed: 7,000 people 'reasonably distributed', initial room occupancies are random numbers (100-200) in the toy case, and node graphs are hand-simplified from floor plans — no real visitor-distribution data, so the 288.7s headline number is illustrative, not validated

Ford-Fulkerson with BFS does not optimize evacuation time per se — maximizing instantaneous outflow each timestep is a greedy myopic heuristic, not proven to minimize total evacuation time; no comparison against an optimal or alternative routing

Max-flow gives flow values on edges but the paper is vague on how those map to actual per-person route instructions and how people are told which way to go (assumption-heavy: signs clear, everyone rational, no stampedes, no counter-flow)

SES model parameters (alpha, beta, gamma weights; critical/max densities) are taken from cited literature with little calibration, and the paper concedes personnel-characteristic effects 'cannot be well described'
The OCR-extracted figures/data tables in this markdown are heavily garbled (e.g. nonsensical radar/scatter value columns), so quantitative figure claims can't be fully verified from this copy
Sensitivity analysis is thin: a single ±5% density perturbation on one parameter, not a multi-parameter or structural sensitivity study
Acknowledged algorithmic caveats: Ford-Fulkerson can fail to terminate on irrational/adversarial capacities (dismissed as ~zero probability), and storage grows with time-resolution
reusable_patterns: Super-source + super-sink virtual nodes to collapse any multi-origin/multi-destination flow problem into a single-source-single-sink max-flow — reusable for evacuation, traffic, supply chains, network routing

Dynamic-capacity max-flow loop: run a static graph algorithm inside a time-step loop and recompute edge capacities from a physical sub-model each iteration — a general recipe for adding dynamics to any static optimizer
Couple a microscopic empirical/physics model (here speed-density curves) to a macroscopic network model through the edge-capacity interface — clean two-scale modeling pattern for MCM

Reuse the algorithm's residual/slack output as a diagnostic: Ford-Fulkerson's leftover residual graph becomes a bottleneck/utilization heatmap at zero extra cost — generalize to 'mine the solver's byproducts for insight'
Validate on a small synthetic instance first (8-node toy building, report runtime), then scale to the real instance (Louvre) — builds judge trust before the big claim
Lead with two roadmap figures (a problem-decomposition mind-map and a method-pipeline flowchart) plus quantified results in the summary — strong MCM structural template
Block-matrix state encoding: assemble the full system state as a sum of semantically-named submatrices (G = corridor + stair + entry + exit) for a self-documenting data structure
Build a complex structure hierarchically: model each component (floor) independently, then compose via explicit linking edges (stairs) into the full system graph
Treat solver speed vs. real-world process speed as a value proposition (pre-compute, run many what-if scenarios) — reframes a performance number as a decision-support benefit
